user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h
## Active selection of the file:
WebServer
## My request for Codex:
we need a session-security-state type of structure to hold web browser session security information
only one session is allowed, even having two browser windows open is not allowed and will simply cause stuff to fail
the handler for "/get_info" needs to
* randomly generate 32 bytes, called the session-challenge, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-challenge, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-client, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-response-from-client, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-server, and remember this
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called the session-salt-from-server, and remember this
* the response from "/get_info" needs to include: session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
on the client (javascript), a similar object needs to track the session security information
the javascript ajax call into "/get_info" needs to:
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called session-salt-from-client, remember this, during the call into "/get_info", this is included
upon receiving a reply from "/get_info", which includes session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server, all of this needs to be remembered
at this point, the user has not entered in a password, we do not have the key to do anything, we cannot generate any of the responses
at some point, the user will enter in a password. When this happens:
* derive the network-key from the password (a copy of `kSaltNetwork` needs to be included in the javascript file)
* derive the session-response-from-client (from the session-challenge and network-key)
* derive the session-response-from-server (from session-response-from-client and network-key)
* validate the computed-session-response-from-server against the received-session-response-from-server
* if invalid, then the password is wrong or the server is malfunctioning
* if valid, continue
* generate 16 bytes randomly as session-salt-from-client
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* derive session-key from the network-key and session-salt
* use ajax to request "/get_cfg", included in the request header are: session-response-from-client, session-salt-from-client
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
* delete the entire `#login_panel` from the page, and blank out any variables that may contain the password. All keys are to be stored securely using WebCrypto if possible
The C++ backend needs to have "/get_cfg" modified to handle all of that
* validate session-response-from-client using network-key and session-challenge
* return error code if not validated
* if validated continue
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* compute the session-key with the network-key and session-salt
* construct the reply with the information as JSON but as a gigantic string
* encrypt the gigantic string using session-key
* send the encrypted reply
When we get the data from "/get_cfg"
* decrypt the data into JSON
* if not authentic, stop and warn the user.
* if authentic, continue
* fill the page elements with data from the JSON
delete `WebServer::authenticateRequest` and all usages of it, later when decryption is implemented, the decryption results will indicate if the request is authentic.
do tasks up to here so I have a chance to review, I'll issue another prompt for "/set_cfg", which would reuse a ton of what has been built up to this point
agent_reply: Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
What changed:
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- `/get_info` now creates the challenge, server salt, server response, resets nonce state, and returns those fields in JSON from [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:263).
- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.
Verification passed:
- `node --check html\script.js`
- `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest`
- targeted `git diff --check`

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the device’s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.
Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
What changed:
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- `/get_info` now creates the challenge, server salt, server response, resets nonce state, and returns those fields in JSON from [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:263).
- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.
Verification passed:
- `node --check html\script.js`
- `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest`
- targeted `git diff --check`

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the device’s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.

user_prompt: ### ask: decrypted download

You’ve helped me benchmark how fast it takes to decrypt a file on-device, it was slow, like a one hour meeting recording would take maybe 20 minutes to decrypt, but, that was mostly because of the microSD card simultaneous reading and writing being a bottleneck.
I want to implement decrypt while downloading. Hopefully this is faster.

The framework for authentication and encrypted communication is already established in the front-end. We’ve also added a unlock button for files that are `*.rec`, right now clicking on them does nothing. This button is shown and hidden appropriately for the given file name and login state.
So in `lib\WifiManager\WebFileHandlers.cpp` we will add a handler for `/decrypted_download`, registered in `lib\WifiManager\WebServer.cpp`

The front-end will construct a JSON object, with “file_name” as a key to indicate which file is being requested, along with a key “action” indicating “download”. This JSON blob is encrypted with the `sessionAesKey` before being sent to `/decrypted_download`. The CPP side of `/decrypted_download` will decrypt it using `uint8_t session_key[]` , failure to decrypt would indicate not being authenticated
Also handle when “action” is “stream”, then the mime-type is one that indicates that it is a `*.wav` audio file. If the key “action” is missing, then use mime-type being octet stream.

If authenticated and the file exists, then stream it back to the client decrypted chunk by decrypted chunk. The indicated download file name should have a `*.wav` extension. The mime-type should be octet stream
I believe as we have known sizes for chunks both encrypted and decrypted, estimating the unencrypted file size should be easy
agent_reply: Implemented decrypted download.
The unlock button now posts an encrypted session-key request to `/decrypted_download` from [html/script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:267), then saves the decrypted response as a `.wav`.
Backend pieces:
- Registered secured POST route in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:909)
- Added handler declarations in [WebFileHandlers.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.h:12)

- Added encrypted request parsing plus chunk-by-chunk `.rec` to WAV streaming in [WebFileHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:458) and [WebFileHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:1293)
Behavior matches the ask:
- Request JSON uses `file_name` plus `action: "download"`, encrypted with `sessionAesKey`
- Server authenticates session headers, decrypts with `session_key`
- GCM decrypt failure returns auth-style failure
- Only `.rec` paths are accepted
- `download` or missing `action` returns `application/octet-stream`
- `stream` returns `audio/wav`
- `Content-Disposition` suggests the same filename with `.wav`
- WAV size is estimated from encrypted chunk count and the decrypted WAV header is patched accordingly

- `node --check html\sec_helpers.js`

- `pio run -e thefly_sec1`
- `pio run -e thefly_sec2 -e thefly_sec3`

agent_reply: Fixed the two blockers you called out.
`/get_info`:
- `wifi_mac` no longer uses `WiFi.macAddress()`, which was returning `00:00:00:00:00:00` on the soft AP path.
- It now reads the ESP MAC directly via `esp_read_mac()`.
- If STA has an IP, it reports `ESP_MAC_WIFI_STA`; otherwise it reports `ESP_MAC_WIFI_SOFTAP`, matching the interface the browser is actually using.
`/list_files.json`:
- Replaced the async chunked directory stream with a simple full JSON response built while the root directory handle is scoped and closed before `request->send()`.
- This should stop the “first load works, later loads are blank” behavior.
Verified:
- `pio run -e thefly_sec1` passes
- `git diff --check` passes, only CRLF warnings
The decrypted-download 404 may still be its own issue, but this should get the file list back so you can click a `.rec` again and we can see the real failure path.

user_prompt: I don't understand, why not just fix what is wrong with the  chunked response method? what is the real problem? the chunked response method is so that we don't blow up memory if the file count gets too long
agent_reply: You’re right. The chunked response is the right shape for `/list_files.json`; my previous full-buffer change was too blunt.
The real likely failure is resource lifetime on aborted reloads:
- `FileListJsonStream` keeps `FsFile m_root` open across async chunk callbacks.
- A browser refresh/devtools reload can abort `/list_files.json` before the chunked response reaches EOF.
- If AsyncWebServer doesn’t destroy that response immediately, the SD directory handle can remain open longer than expected.
- Subsequent `/list_files.json` calls may then fail/act empty because the previous directory stream is still alive.
I restored the chunked response and fixed cleanup instead:
- Added `FileListJsonStream::close()`
- Destructor uses `close()`
- Normal EOF uses `close()`
- `request->onDisconnect(...)` now closes the stream immediately on aborted/reloaded requests
- If `beginChunkedResponse()` fails, it also closes the stream
Verified `pio run -e thefly_sec1` passes and `git diff --check` is clean apart from CRLF warnings.

agent_reply: I made the next fix pass and rebuilt `thefly_sec1` successfully.

For `/list_files.json`, I kept the chunked response. The change is that a new file-list request now forcibly closes any previous active file-list stream before opening the SD root again: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:528). That should address the “first load works, second load blank” case if the stale chunked response is keeping the directory handle/state alive.

For decrypted download, I did not paper over it by changing response mode. I added serial diagnostics at the exact places where the stream can die early: seek failure, short SD read, GCM auth failure, audio chunk failure, and final early-stop report: [DecryptedDownload.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/DecryptedDownload.cpp:617).
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec1` passes.
If the decrypted download still stops around 65.58 KB, the new serial log should now say whether it is a short SD read, bad chunk/auth, or a seek/math issue.

user_prompt: Make a `lib\WifiManager\AsyncFsManager.cpp` and its associated header file
It needs public functions
- init, called from any activation of Wi-Fi
- reset walk, reset iterator for file listing, should always work no matter what state
- walk one, return one file from directory, the next call should return the next file, return something to indicate end of list when needed, automatically restart the walk if something is wrong
- close walk
- open file for download, closes any other file or anything else that might conflict, reset seek pointer
- read file chunk
- close file
critically, all of these need to be thread safe
web server code can only use methods within this manager, add thread safe functions to this manager as needed
use it to attempt to fix the problem with listing files and the decrypted download
agent_reply: Implemented `AsyncFsManager` and routed the web SD access through it.
What changed:
- Added [AsyncFsManager.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.h:19) and [AsyncFsManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:93).
- It owns the active walk handle and active download handle behind a recursive FreeRTOS mutex.
- `resetWalk`, `walkOne`, `closeWalk`, `openFileForDownload`, `readFileChunk`, and `closeFile` are all serialized.
- Opening a download closes any active walk; resetting a walk closes any active download.
- Added thread-safe helpers for web upload/delete/readiness: `writeFileChunk`, `removeFile`, `isFile`, `isReady`.
I also wired:
- `/list_files.json` to use `AsyncFsManager::resetWalk()` and `walkOne()` instead of holding its own `FsFile`: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:221)
- `/download_file` to use `openFileForDownload()` / `readFileChunk()`: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:413)
- decrypted download to use the manager for encrypted recording reads: [DecryptedDownload.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/DecryptedDownload.cpp:505)

Implemented `AsyncFsManager` and routed the web SD access through it.
What changed:
- Added [AsyncFsManager.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.h:19) and [AsyncFsManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:93).
- It owns the active walk handle and active download handle behind a recursive FreeRTOS mutex.
- `resetWalk`, `walkOne`, `closeWalk`, `openFileForDownload`, `readFileChunk`, and `closeFile` are all serialized.
- Opening a download closes any active walk; resetting a walk closes any active download.
- Added thread-safe helpers for web upload/delete/readiness: `writeFileChunk`, `removeFile`, `isFile`, `isReady`.
I also wired:
- `/list_files.json` to use `AsyncFsManager::resetWalk()` and `walkOne()` instead of holding its own `FsFile`: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:221)

- decrypted download to use the manager for encrypted recording reads: [DecryptedDownload.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/DecryptedDownload.cpp:505)
- Wi-Fi activation reset via `AsyncFsManager::init()`: [WifiManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:397)

and then file listing started to fail
agent_reply: I patched the plain download path.
The important fixes:
- Plain downloads now use a fixed `Content-Length` response instead of open-ended chunked transfer.
- `Content-Disposition` now includes the actual filename, so the browser should save `whatever.rec` instead of `download_file`.
- `AsyncFsManager::readFileChunk()` now clamps reads to the known file size and closes the active download handle at EOF, so a completed download should not poison later `/list_files.json` walks.
- Starting a plain download now closes any active file-list stream first.
Build passes: `pio run -e thefly_sec1`.
This should directly address the mysterious filename, wrong size, and post-download listing failure in one place.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp
## Active selection of the file:
"/file_upload"
## My request for Codex:
does the web server handlers for "/file_upload" correctly use `lib\WifiManager\AsyncFsManager.cpp` such that the uploads are thread safe?
agent_reply: Short answer: **partly yes, but not completely**.
`/file_upload` does route upload bytes through `AsyncFsManager`:
- Route registration: [WebServer.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:198)
- Body/part callbacks call `write_file_upload_bytes()`: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:168)
- That calls `AsyncFsManager::writeFileChunk()`: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:529)

- `writeFileChunk()` takes the recursive FS mutex, closes active walk/download state, opens/writes/syncs/closes the file: [AsyncFsManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:307)
So it is thread-safe **against other code that also goes through `AsyncFsManager`**, such as web file listing/download/delete.
The caveats are the important bit:
1. The lock is per chunk, not per upload. Two simultaneous uploads to the same `file_name` can interleave or truncate each other because each first chunk uses `O_TRUNC`, later chunks use `O_APPEND`.

2. It is not globally thread-safe against other filesystem users. A lot of code uses `MicroSdCard::fs()` / `FsFile` directly, including recording, playback/delete, cloud upload, firmware update, and FTP. Those do not share the `AsyncFsManager` mutex.
So: **the handler correctly uses `AsyncFsManager`, but uploads are only thread-safe within the web/AsyncFsManager lane, not as a whole-device filesystem transaction.**

- `AsyncFsManager` now has `openFileForUpload()`, `writeUploadFileChunk()`, `closeUploadFile()`, and upload session IDs in [AsyncFsManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp).

- `/file_upload` now opens once on the first chunk, writes chunks sequentially by offset, verifies the final raw-body byte count, then syncs/closes once at finish in [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp).
- Client disconnect cleanup now only closes the matching upload session, so a late disconnect cannot kill a newer upload.
- Web UI upload timeout went from 2 minutes to 10 minutes in [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js).
- `inc/web_assets.h` was regenerated by the build.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec1`: success.
This should fix the “partial file then 500” behavior if it was caused by per-chunk SD sync/open/close overhead or browser timeout.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp
## Active selection of the file:
"/file_upload"
## My request for Codex:
the first file, 5MB, was successful, the second and third both failed and not even created on the card, the dialog says "File write failed" but interestingly the progress bar is 100%
agent_reply: Yep, that 100% progress bar is only “browser finished sending bytes to the ESP,” not “ESP finished committing to SD.” The old message hid whether the failure was open/write/close.
I tightened this up:
- Raw uploads now preallocate the file when `Content-Length` is known.
- Uploads sync every 512 KB instead of relying on one big final flush.
- Final close now truncates preallocated size to actual bytes and checks `close()`.
- `removeFile()` no longer blindly closes whatever upload is active.
- UI/server errors are now more specific: `File open failed`, `File chunk write failed`, `File close failed`, or `Incomplete file upload`.
- `inc/web_assets.h` regenerated from the updated web JS.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec1`: success.
If it still fails, the new dialog text plus serial log SD error codes should tell us exactly which phase is breaking.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp
## My request for Codex:
see the previous git commit, did I do anything wrong in the merge resolution?
agent_reply: Yes, I found two likely merge-resolution mistakes.
**Findings**

- High: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:557) registers `request->onDisconnect([] { endWebUpload(); })`, then [line 586](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:586) registers another `onDisconnect` for `cancelUploadFile(session_id)`. In this ESPAsyncWebServer, `onDisconnect()` is a setter, so the second callback overwrites the first. If an upload disconnects after the file is opened, `g_web_upload_active` can stay true forever, and [guiShouldYield](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:115) will keep the GUI yielding. Fix by using one combined callback that cancels the upload and ends web upload.

- Medium: [index.html](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html:45) kept `<a class="delete-link" href="/delete_file?file_name=">`, but [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:249) treats it like a button: sets `disabled`, assigns `onclick`, and does a `fetch(POST)` without `preventDefault()`. Clicking can still follow the stale empty `href`, potentially navigating away or racing/canceling the POST. Best fix is to restore these to `<button type="button" class="delete-link">`, matching the JS side.
**Other Checks**

No unresolved conflict markers found. `node --check html/script.js` passes. `git diff --check HEAD^1 HEAD` reports trailing whitespace in `lib/ESP-FTP-Server-Lib/src/FTPCommand.h`, but that looks like imported/vendor code rather than the conflict area.
I did not change files; this was review-only.

**Findings**

- High: [WebFileHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:557) registers `request->onDisconnect([] { endWebUpload(); })`, then [line 586](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp:586) registers another `onDisconnect` for `cancelUploadFile(session_id)`. In this ESPAsyncWebServer, `onDisconnect()` is a setter, so the second callback overwrites the first. If an upload disconnects after the file is opened, `g_web_upload_active` can stay true forever, and [guiShouldYield](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/AsyncFsManager.cpp:115) will keep the GUI yielding. Fix by using one combined callback that cancels the upload and ends web upload.

- Medium: [index.html](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html:45) kept `<a class="delete-link" href="/delete_file?file_name=">`, but [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:249) treats it like a button: sets `disabled`, assigns `onclick`, and does a `fetch(POST)` without `preventDefault()`.
